html:
XVI

Mr.¬†T. P. Randall was extremely solicitous in his attitude toward his charming client. She had telephoned him at ten, and they had agreed on a business conference at one-thirty.

He was tall, fifty-five, well-fed; grizzled at the temples. The tailor who had made his waistcoat might have succeeded as a sculptor. He rose, as she entered his padded leather and dark mahogany sanctum, dignifiedly offered his hand, bowed to the top of her close-fitting grey hat from his considerable altitude, helped off the grey fur coat from the grey gown, drew out the throne-like chair for her, seated her, walked around the table majestically, sat down in his swivel-chair, folded his big, pink, newly manicured hands on the bare desk, and said again that he was glad to see her. He mentally doubted his assertion, however, and looked more than a little troubled.

Considering it highly important that he should lead the conversation into safe channels, he talked of Paris, where he had once spent a fortnight, and of Venice, where, he declared, he would like to live; but it was obvious, from the restlessness with which she chafed the backs of her grey gloves, that she hadnвАЩt come to hear his impressions of Europe.

At the first semicolon, she leaned forward in her big chair.

вАЬYou had a long talk with Mr.¬†Brent about my affairs.вАЭ

T. P.вБ†вАФhe was known as T. P. throughout the Fourth National organizationвБ†вАФdrew an anxious smile. Now, why the devil hadnвАЩt he been told that he was supposed to have talked with this rapscallion Brent? He had been under the impression that Brent was to be presumed as having had all his business with him by correspondence.

вАЬUmm!вАЭ murmured T. P., deep in his throat. He gave it just that quality of indeterminateness which might make it pass either for an affirmative, or a mere receipt of information already in hand, or a promise that presently he would discourse at more length about the matter when she had quite finished her remarks.

But she was not going to be contented with his вАЬUmm.вАЭ He saw that at a glance.

Mrs.¬†Hudson smiledвБ†вАФa bit roguishly.

вАЬThere was one man you had to look up to; is it not so?вАЭ

Her syntax was unfortunately under continental influence. It made the query difficult to evadeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ My eye!вБ†вАФwas this innocent child with the wide blue eyes leading DetroitвАЩs most resourceful side-stepper into a trap?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, heвАЩd follow along, and see what came of it. Of a sudden he remembered; he brightened; he tossed up an outspread hand.

вАЬRather!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Uncommonly tall!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Your cousin, I think.вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ agreed Mrs.¬†Hudson.

T. P. took a long breath, exhaled it luxuriously, and felt relieved.

вАЬMust be six feet three, isnвАЩt he?вАЭ

вАЬAbout my height,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hudson. вАЬButвБ†вАФhe is my cousin.вАЭ

вАЬOhвБ†вАФof course!вАЭ T. P. laughed, boisterously. вАЬOf course! You knew I was jesting, of course!вАЭ

She did not join in his merriment.

вАЬOdd that you should have forgotten!вАЭ she said meaningly.

When in doubt about what next to do, T. P. always fell back on the didactic style. He could stun and bewilder with his voluminous vocabulary of technical terms relating to the upper ether of large finance. He settled sternly to it, ignoring their brief exchange of exploratory thrusts, and discoursed of stocks. Most industrials were good now; motors especially; Axion most assuredly. She could be confident that her money was prudently placed. Moreover, she could sleep soundly oвАЩ nights while the Fourth National looked out for her interestsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ AndвБ†вАФby the wayвБ†вАФhe wanted to show her about through this fine new building before she left, if there was timeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Not quite satisfied with her expression, he launched upon an oration of some length, rumbling wisely of economic trends, cycles, the periodicity of financial mutations now happily stabilized by the Federal Reserve. At the first full stop, she said:

вАЬI would like to see my stock certificates.вАЭ

вАЬTo be sure, Mrs.¬†Hudson! Of course!вАЭ

T. P.вАЩs tone was paternal. Inwardly he chuckled. If this amazingly good looking young widow had hopes of learning how she had become possessed of her Axion Motors by inspecting her stock certificates, she was about to be disappointed. She had caught him napping in respect to his relations with her neвАЩer-do-well cousin, and it was up to him to take the next trick in this little game. WellвБ†вАФshe would discover nothing new, bearing on the question, from these certificates. HadnвАЩt he told Riley to hustle those shares of Doctor MerrickвАЩs back to the Axion office to be reissued in the name of Mrs.¬†Hudson? Of course!

HoweverвБ†вАФjust to make doubly sure. He quite distinctly remembered having written to Blair, the transfer agent of the Axion Motor Corporation, notifying him that, beginning at once, all future dividends on that block of stock were to be forwarded to the address of Mrs.¬†Hudson, who now owned them, and that the certificates would be brought over to him for re-issuance. Surely he had remembered to tell Riley about that. To be on the safe side, heвАЩd inquire.

вАЬIвАЩll send for them,вАЭ continued T. P., beaming amiably. вАЬExcuse me, please.вАЭ

He was detained in the adjoining office for fully five minutes, and when he returned he was mopping his expansive brow with a large, monogrammed handkerchief. Resuming his chair, he smiled, not very happily, and said:

вАЬIt may take quite a little time. Had we known you would want to see them, they would have been ready for you. We have such things pretty carefully stowed away, you know.вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Helen, comprehendingly, вАЬyou would have, of course.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs rather a pity to keep you waiting so long,вАЭ regretted T. P. Why the devil couldnвАЩt the woman say, вАЬOhвБ†вАФnever mind about them, then.вАЭ

вАЬI can wait,вАЭ she replied, settling comfortably in her big chair.

T. P. drummed on the desk with anxious fingers.

вАЬYou know we have them, of course, or you wouldnвАЩt be getting your dividends.вАЭ

вАЬOhвБ†вАФcertainly.вАЭ

вАЬTheyвАЩre just like any otherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ YouвАЩve seen stock certificates, havenвАЩt you?вАЭ He was still amiable, but he was growing desperate.

вАЬYesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and I would like to see these!вАЭ She glanced at her watch.

There was nothing more that T. P. could do about it. He pushed a button, gave an order, tried to be cheerful, tried to be nonchalant; but the conversation was unsatisfactory. Neither of them had the slightest interest in it.

At length the certificates arrived, and he pushed them across the desk.

She slipped the top one from under the broad rubber band, spread it out, turned it over, and noted the endorsement whereby it had been transferred from Robert Merrick to Helen Hudson.

вАЬThank you!вАЭ she said, rising. вАЬThat will be all, I think. I shall be in again tomorrow to talk further with you.вАЭ

T. P. did not waste time, immediately his client had left. He took up the telephone, and in his best bank manner told the switchboard to get him Doctor Merrick at Brightwood Hospital. The contact was no sooner made than T. P. went off the deep end, without ceremony, and reported what had happened. The answer he received consisted of a few words not properly used over the telephone, which really has to draw the line somewhere.

вАЬNowвБ†вАФDocвБ†вАФyouвАЩve got to keep your shirt on! I tell you it absolutely couldnвАЩt be avoided! It was either thatвБ†вАФor tell her we didnвАЩt have the stuff at all! Would that have improved the situation any?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Hell!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ SheвАЩs suspected all along. You might вАЩaвАЩ known she would! That womanвАЩs no ninny!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What say?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ WhyвБ†вАФdamned if I know, Doc. She left hereвБ†вАФprontoвБ†вАФshot out of a gunвБ†вАФa bit flustered, maybeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ NoвБ†вАФI donвАЩt know, I tell you. Perhaps she was. If so, I suppose youвАЩll soon find it out!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, IвАЩm sorry as you are, Doc; but you might вАЩaвАЩвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

There was a metallic click that made T. P. wince. He laid the instrument back on its rack, opened the left lower drawer of the desk, took out a bottle and a glass; poured, swallowed, sizzled and shuddered; replaced the bottle, shut the drawer, lighted a cigar, pushed a button.

вАЬRileyвБ†вАФput those certificates back where you got вАЩemвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and if anybody wants me importantly, call me at the Athletic Club. IвАЩve a headache.вАЭ

Not often was Helen Hudson a victim of emotional stampede. Her poise was not a pose; neither was it arrived at by effort. It was native to her.

This afternoon she simply tossed the reins of discretion upon the neck of her indignation and abandoned herself to the tempestuous rush of it. Mentally at full gallop, she hurled herself at Brightwood.

It was as if a huge, ugly cauldron of anxieties, perplexities, forebodings, misgivings and suspicions, which had been simmering and bubbling for all the dragging months, had suddenly reached that stage of the brewing when it was time to pour.

A taxi waited at the door of the bank as she emerged, half-blind with humiliation. She walked swiftly to it, gave the driver an order, and sat tense throughout the journey.

This abominable Merrick had placed her in an impossible positionвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No matter about the intentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He had doubtless enjoyed his GalahadingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But he had made her his pensioner; treated her like an irresponsible child; helplessly loaded her with an obligation she would probably be unable to dischargeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, she could disavow her willingness to accept anything further at his hands! She could return the capital of it, at once; and set to work toward a replacement of what she had spent.

Exactly what she was going to say to Bobby Merrick when she saw him, Helen had not yet determined clearly. Of one thing she was sure: she would denounce his officious meddling with her affairs, and let him know exactly where he stood in her regardвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He should have it all back!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She pressed her shaking fingers against her eyes and tried to cool her cheeks with the back of her gloves.

Barely conscious of the journey, the unhappy girl stepped out of the cab as it stopped in front of the hospital, ordered the driver to waitвБ†вАФshe would not be longвБ†вАФand quickly pattered up the broad concrete walk between masses of formal shrubbery coated with glistening ice.

At the desk in the snug little lobby, she inquired for Mrs.¬†Ashford, and was shown into her office, where she quite took that pretty ladyвАЩs breath away with her exotic beauty.

вАЬWhyвБ†вАФwhat a joy!вАЭ cried Nancy, putting forward both hands in greeting. вАЬI knew you were in town, and have been so anxious to see you!вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Helen breathlessly, and with an effort to steady her voice, вАЬI do want to have a good visit with youвБ†вАФand I shallвБ†вАФsoon. But notвБ†вАФjust nowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I find I have a rather sudden errand with Doctor Merrick. Could I see him?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Is he here?вАЭ

He was here, and she could see him. Nancy believed he had just now finished an operation, and would probably be at liberty. She would send him in, and they could talk in her office.

Nancy went out, her own heart beating rapidly, and closed the door behind her. For a while, Helen fidgeted on the little divan, fumbled with her pocketbook, latching and unlatching and latching, tapping her little grey-shod toes impatiently on the rug; then, unable to sit still another instant, she rose, walked to the window, and stood gazing preoccupiedly at the street, her fingers busy with her coral beads.

At length, the door was quietly opened and as quietly closed, and she was aware of his presence in the room. She knew he was standing there, just inside the door, expectant, waiting for her to turn and face himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Why in the world didnвАЩt she?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Would he cross the little room, and approach herвБ†вАФspeak to her?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Perhaps notвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But why didnвАЩt she turn quickly and confront him?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She had asked for this interview, hadnвАЩt she?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She had sent for him to come to her, hadnвАЩt she?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What ever ailed her, anyway?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The difficult thing about it was that she had delayed turning about to face him!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Every second that passed made the situation more tryingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

After a young eternity, he spokeвБ†вАФrather unsteadily.

вАЬYou wanted to see me?вАЭ

His quiet query broke the spell for her. She turned quickly, and, leaning against the window, put her outspread hands upon the sill, in a pose that Bobby sorrowfully interpreted as a sort of back-to-the-wall defence; but not defiance. Her head was bowed; her eyes to the floor. It was so thoroughly against his wish and hope that whatever he had done for her should put her into this attitude.

Helen was dismayed at her own sensations. Ten minutes ago, she had been ready to do violence. When she had stepped to that window, she had been aflame with a passionate anxiety to call him hard names; to hurt him, somehow; to let him taste a little of the humiliation he had put upon herвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What had happened to her?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She felt deserted even by her own rageвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ WellвБ†вАФshe could not stand there silently any longer.

She lifted heavy eyes to meet BobbyвАЩs.

вАЬI must have a talk with you,вАЭ she said, in that throaty contralto he so keenly remembered; a timbre that seemed to set up all manner of curious vibrations in him.

вАЬWonвАЩt you shake hands?вАЭ he begged.

вАЬNo need!вАЭ she said, with a little gesture of futility.

вАЬThenвБ†вАФwill you sit down?вАЭ

вАЬThank youвБ†вАФno. I think I can say itвБ†вАФquickly!вАЭ

Bobby leaned against a corner of Nancy AshfordвАЩs desk, folded his arms and listened.

вАЬI have just discovered that everything I have in the world isвБ†вАФis yours. I have been living, for some time, as your dependentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I didnвАЩt know. IвАЩm sure you will believe I didnвАЩt knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬOf course you didnвАЩt know! You have nothing to blame yourself withвБ†вАФin this matter.вАЭ

She went on as if she had not heard.

вАЬThe very clothes I have on meвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

She lowered her head and covered her eyes with her outspread fingers.

Bobby could stand no more. With his heart furiously pounding, he stepped quickly to her, and took her hands in his. For the tiniest instant, she permitted his impulsive gesture of sympathy, and then withdrew from him, shaking her head.

вАЬNo! No! I didnвАЩt come here to be pitied!вАЭ Her voice was firmer now, and there was a note of rising impatience in it. вАЬIвАЩve been pitiedвБ†вАФquite long enough! All I came to tell you is that all your money at the bank I am going to turn back to you; and, as fast as I can possibly earn it, I shall pay you every dollar I have spent.вАЭ

Bobby drew a deep sigh of regret, stepped back, and leaned his weight against the desk, his eyes brooding.

вАЬIвАЩm so sorry,вАЭ he said slowly. вАЬYou seeвБ†вАФthe circumstances were very strange. I wanted to spare you, if I could, a misfortune that would bring you some unhappiness. I guess I went about it the wrong wayвБ†вАФbut I meant it all right. WonвАЩt you believe that?вАЭ

For a second, their eyes met in a look that each remembered later, when, that night, the episode was reviewed, inch by inch, and word by word, on sleepless pillowsвБ†вАФBobby solemnly wondering whether, had he taken her in his arms at that moment, their difficulties might have been solvedвБ†вАФHelen remorsefully chiding herself for what she feared was a serious disclosure of a feeling she had tried to batter into submission.

вАЬPerhaps you did,вАЭ she admitted, tugging herself loose from his eyes. вАЬBut it doesnвАЩt make my position any more endurable. I donвАЩt propose to be pensioned by you! IвАЩm going to give all your money backвБ†вАФthe capital, tomorrowвБ†вАФthe amount I have used, at the earliest moment I can earn it!вАЭ

вАЬYou mustnвАЩt do that!вАЭ

BobbyвАЩs voice was stern, commanding. He stood erect and faced her determinedly.

вАЬThereвАЩs more to this than you are aware ofвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ more than I dare tell you! The lives of many people would unquestionably be affected! Whatever you may decide to do with that money, you canвАЩt give it back to me! I wonвАЩt take it! I canвАЩt take itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ because you see, IвАЩve used it all up!вАЭ

Helen glanced up quickly, her eyes wide with amazement. She swallowed convulsively.

вАЬWвАСhвАСaвАСt?вАЭ she whispered. вАЬWhat is that you say?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve used it all up!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you know what that means?вАЭ

вАЬNo! Tell me! What does it mean?вАЭ

вАЬSit down,вАЭ he said gently. вАЬIвАЩll try to explainвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs not easy, though.вАЭ

She walked with some reluctance to the little divan and sat.

вАЬPerhaps you never had it called to your attention,вАЭвБ†вАФBobby was feeling his way with cautionвБ†вАФвАЬthat there is sometimes a strange relation between the voluntary, secret bestowal of a gift, without expectation of any return or reward, and certain significant results that accrue from it in the experience of the giverвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Now I am not sure that this money I was so happy to put at your disposal is not that kind of an investment. Shortly after I arranged for it, something of quite tremendous importance occurredвБ†вАФsomething that dared not be trifled with by either of usвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs almost hopeless to try to make it convincing to you, I knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ CanвАЩt you just take my word for itвБ†вАФand trust meвБ†вАФdear?вАЭ

Helen flushed deeply, and roseвБ†вАФher eyes flashing.

вАЬNoвБ†вАФyouвАЩre not dealing squarely with me!вАЭ she retorted hotly. вАЬAnd IвАЩm notвБ†вАФyourвБ†вАФdear! You have humiliated me! There are many things I have wanted to know, and you seem to be able to clear up some mysteries; but you are plainly not disposed to do so. IвАЩm going now. I shall arrange at the bank about the money. AndвБ†вАФthe rest of itвБ†вАФthe part IвАЩve spentвБ†вАФI shall pay that back! You can depend on it!вАЭ She was at the door, her hand on the knob.

Bobby stepped swiftly to her side and covered her hand with his own.

вАЬListen!вАЭ he demanded soberly. вАЬIt is quite important, both for your sake and mine, that we do not set every tongue in this place wagging with the gossip that we met here to have a row. YouвАЩre ready to go stamping out through the office, rosy with rage. Much curiosity will be aroused and explanations will be in order.вАЭ

вАЬThen you can make them! I do not feel that I owe anybody an explanation. If you think you do, that is your affair! Let me go, please.вАЭ

Bobby did not remove his detaining hand.

вАЬMy dear,вАЭ he said, scarcely above a whisper, вАЬI appeal to your good sportsmanship! GrantedвБ†вАФthat you have cause to be indignant. GrantedвБ†вАФthat I have blunderingly placed you in an awkward predicament. Let us at least keep our misunderstanding to ourselves. Please! Compose yourselfвБ†вАФand weвАЩll go out to face these people as if there were no trouble between us. WouldnвАЩt that be ever so much better?вАЭ

She hesitated for a long moment; looked up searchingly into his eyes, like a bewildered little child, and finally replied:

вАЬAgreed.вАЭ

He released her hand and she walked to the window, took out a tiny vanity case and consulted her reflection, Bobby regarding her with repentant eyes. How wretchedly he had bungled everythingвБ†вАФeverything!

Presently she turned and faced him calmly, like a stranger.

вАЬIвАЩm quite ready, if you are.вАЭ

Bobby hesitated.

вАЬButвБ†вАФreallyвБ†вАФdonвАЩt you thinkвБ†вАФвАЭ he stammered shyly, вАЬthat a wee bit of a smile might help toвБ†вАФtoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll attend to that when we need it!вАЭ

He opened the door and signed for her to precede him. At that instant, she became another person, gracious, smiling.

Nancy Ashford, who had been hovering about, with curiosity and some anxiety, met them. She drew a quick breath, apparently of relief.

вАЬIвАЩm so glad you two have found some occasion to become acquainted at last,вАЭ she exclaimed, searching their faces eagerly.

вАЬYesвБ†вАФisnвАЩt it?вАЭ responded Helen, slightly confused over her cue. вАЬDoctor Merrick and I have been talking of so many interesting thingsвБ†вАФsome of them quite mystifying, IвАЩm afraid.вАЭ

Ah!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Good!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ So that was it!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Mystifying things!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Mrs.¬†Hudson had been asking questionsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Somehow she had learnedвБ†вАФby a chance word, perhapsвБ†вАФthat Bobby Merrick was in a position to clear up some of the strange riddles bequeathed to her by her husbandвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ SoвБ†вАФthat was what had brought them togetherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Good!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Nancy was radiant.

вАЬI hope I may soon have a little visit with you, Mrs.¬†Ashford,вАЭ continued Helen rather breathlessly. вАЬTodayвБ†вАФIвАЩm in something of a rushвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Important errands.вАЭ

At the sound of the familiar voice, just outside the door of her little filing-room, Joyce came bounding into the circle with a shrill exclamation of surprise and delight and a torrent of questions.

вАЬWhy, whatever brought you here, darling? Why didnвАЩt you tell me you were coming so soon? And you and Doctor Merrick have actually met! How jolly? OhвБ†вАФnow we can have that dinner we talked about! LetвАЩs do it tonight! We four! My party! And IвАЩll get tickets for Jasmine! What do you all say? Can you come, Mrs.¬†Ashford?вАЭ

Nancy swiftly sought HelenвАЩs eyes, and thought she detected a faint expression of annoyance. Should she encourage the dinner project? It was an awkward moment.

Suddenly sensing her own obligation to take a cordial interest in JoyceвАЩs proffer of hospitality, Helen smiled inquiringly, and Nancy replied, вАЬI should be very happy, Joyce. Thank you.вАЭ

вАЬAnd you can come, too, canвАЩt you, Bobby?вАЭ persisted Joyce.

He studied HelenвАЩs face for a brief second, and found his heart pounding when she glanced up unmistakably interested to hear his reply.

вАЬIвАЩll be very glad to come, Joyce.вАЭ

Helen glanced at her watch.

вАЬI must be going,вАЭ she said determinedly. вАЬIвАЩll see you all this evening, then.вАЭ

The three of them accompanied her to the big, glass-panelled doorsвБ†вАФBobby at her elbow, obviously proposing to see her to the waiting taxi. The pair descended the snowy steps, his hand on her arm, both conscious that Nancy and Joyce were still standing just inside the door, observant.

вАЬWe must talk,вАЭ said Bobby cheerfully. вАЬThis game isnвАЩt over yet.вАЭ

вАЬTrue,вАЭ agreed Helen, turning toward him with a smile that fairly dizzied him. вАЬAnd from all indications, youвАЩve let us in for a whole evening of this delightful recreation! Whatever made you say you would come to that wretched dinner Joyce contrived to plan? Some more of your good sportsmanship, I suppose!вАЭ

Bobby was contrite. His face was overspread with it.

вАЬDonвАЩt look like that!вАЭ she commanded, her tone oddly out of step with her smile. вАЬTheyвАЩll think we are quarrelling!вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФвАЭ glumly, вАЬarenвАЩt we?вАЭ

She laughed.

вАЬAs a dramatist, you seem to be better as a producer than an actor!вАЭ

вАЬBut, really, I was going to refuse the invitation. And then, when I happened to glance at you, you seemed soвБ†вАФso friendly about itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬWhat had you thought I might do? Scowl at you? It was your own suggestion that we appear to be on good terms. And nowвБ†вАФwell, you have taken advantage of meвБ†вАФas usual.вАЭ She was still smiling. They had reached the kerb. The taxi-driver was churning his engine.

вАЬIвАЩll contrive some excuse,вАЭ decided Bobby, weakly. вАЬIвАЩm sorry.вАЭ

вАЬNo! You canвАЩt do that. WeвАЩre in for it, and weвАЩll see it through; and I can promise you that my own feelings will not be apparent to anybody.вАЭ She hesitated for a moment, and added, вАЬNot even to you! IвАЩll guarantee not to spoil your dinner.вАЭ

Bobby opened the door and helped her in. The warm grasp of his hand on her arm vexed herвБ†вАФthrilled her. Safe in her seat, she no longer felt under compulsion to make further show of amiability. The smile had vanished. He held out his hand, and she, annoyed, was obliged to accept it. He held it tightly.

вАЬGoodbye, dear,вАЭ he said tenderly. вАЬPlease donвАЩt think too badly of me. I have blundered, terribly, butвБ†вАФmy dearвБ†вАФI do love you so!вАЭ
